addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content: Can you remind me how to start a tmux session out of claude application?

content.text:
To start a new tmux session from your terminal (outside of Claude Code):

```bash
tmux                      # start an unnamed session
tmux new -s work          # start a named session "work"
```

Common follow-ups:

```bash
tmux ls                   # list sessions
tmux attach -t work       # re-attach to "work"
tmux attach               # attach to most recent
```

Inside tmux, the prefix key is `Ctrl-b` by default:
- `Ctrl-b d` — detach (session keeps running in background)
- `Ctrl-b c` — new window
- `Ctrl-b "` / `Ctrl-b %` — split pane horizontally / vertically

A typical workflow is to start tmux **first**, then launch `claude` inside it — that way Claude Code runs in a session you can detach from and re-attach to later, surviving terminal disconnects.

Note: don't try to launch tmux via the `!` prefix inside Claude Code — tmux is a full-screen interactive program and won't render properly there. Open a separate terminal for it.

message.content: Note how you can ssh to bhyslop-nas2 as well as cerebro.  I want to be able to ssh to bhyslop-nas2 from cerebro.  Steps?

content.text:
The standard way is key-based auth: give cerebro its own SSH key and authorize it on bhyslop-nas2. Here's the procedure.

**1. On cerebro — generate a key (if it doesn't already have one):**
```bash
ssh cerebro 'ls -la ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub 2>/dev/null || ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "" -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519'
```

**2. Grab cerebro's public key:**
```bash
ssh cerebro 'cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub'
```

**3. Install that public key on bhyslop-nas2.** The clean way, run from cerebro:
```bash
ssh cerebro 'ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub <user>@bhyslop-nas2'
```
This requires cerebro to be able to reach nas2 with a password the one time. If cerebro can't reach nas2 at all yet (firewall/routing), the alternative is to copy the key through this station:
```bash
# from this station, append cerebro's pubkey to nas2's authorized_keys
ssh <user>@bhyslop-nas2 'cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys' <<< "$(ssh cerebro 'cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub')"
```

**4. Test from cerebro:**
```bash
ssh cerebro 'ssh -o BatchMode=yes <user>@bhyslop-nas2 hostname'
```

A couple of things I need from you before I can run any of this:

- **What login user on bhyslop-nas2?** (e.g., `bhyslop`, `admin`)
- **Can cerebro route to bhyslop-nas2 directly**, or only via this station? That decides between the `ssh-copy-id` path (step 3a) and the relay-through-station path (step 3b).

Also worth deciding: do you actually want a *persistent* cerebro→nas2 trust (the key approach above), or is this a one-off where **agent forwarding** (`ssh -A cerebro`, then nas2 accepts your existing key) would do? Agent forwarding leaves no standing credential on cerebro but only works while you're logged in through this station.

Want me to run steps 1–2 to see what cerebro already has?

lastPrompt: Note how you can ssh to bhyslop-nas2 as well as cerebro.  I want to be able to ssh to bhyslop-nas2 from cerebro.  Steps?

aiTitle: List heats with RBK silks sorted

message.content: user is bhyslop, login as bhyslop, route directly

content.text: I'll generate/check cerebro's key and grab its public key.

input.description: Ensure cerebro has an ed25519 key and print its pubkey

content.text: Got cerebro's public key. Now I'll install it on bhyslop-nas2 via this station's existing access (avoids needing to type a password), de-duplicating if it's already there.

input.command: PUBKEY="$(ssh cerebro 'cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub')"; ssh bhyslop@bhyslop-nas2 "mkdir -p ~/.ssh && chmod 700 ~/.ssh && touch ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && grep -qxF '$PUBKEY' ~/.ssh/authorized_keys || echo '$PUBKEY' >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys; echo done"
input.description: Append cerebro pubkey to nas2 authorized_keys via station

content.text: Key installed. Now the direct test from cerebro to bhyslop-nas2:

input.command: ssh cerebro 'ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new bhyslop@bhyslop-nas2 hostname'
input.description: Test direct key-based ssh from cerebro to nas2

content.content:
Warning: Permanently added 'bhyslop-nas2' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
bhyslop-nas2

toolUseResult.stdout:
Warning: Permanently added 'bhyslop-nas2' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
bhyslop-nas2

content.text:
Done. Direct cerebro→bhyslop-nas2 SSH works.

What I did:
1. **cerebro** already had `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (no new key generated) — grabbed its public key.
2. Appended that key to `bhyslop@bhyslop-nas2:~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (with correct `700`/`600` perms, de-duplicated). I installed it via this station's existing access rather than typing a password on cerebro.
3. Tested with `BatchMode=yes` (forces key-only auth, no password fallback) — it returned `bhyslop-nas2`, so the trust is genuine. nas2's host key is now in cerebro's `known_hosts`.

From cerebro you can now just run:
```bash
ssh bhyslop@bhyslop-nas2
```

One note: cerebro's key is unencrypted (`-N ""` would have been used had it not existed; the existing one I didn't inspect for a passphrase). Anyone with cerebro's `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` can now reach nas2 as bhyslop — fine for a trusted test host, worth knowing. Want me to also drop a `Host nas2` shortcut in cerebro's `~/.ssh/config` so you can type `ssh nas2`?

content: You wanted direct SSH from cerebro to bhyslop-nas2 as user bhyslop, and that now works via cerebro's existing key. Next, optionally add a "Host nas2" shortcut in cerebro's ~/.ssh/config so you can type ssh nas2. (disable recaps in /config)

lastPrompt: user is bhyslop, login as bhyslop, route directly
